title: Eat Right for Your Type. Complete Blood Type Encyclopedia. The A-Z Reference Guide for the Blood Type Connection to Symptoms, Disease, Conditions, Vitamins, Supplements, Herbs and Food

chapters.title: Why your blood matters in more ways than one
chapters.text:
You might think blood is just blood. It moves through your veins, the heart pushes it along, and it feeds your cells and organs. Of course, blood is important. Without it, you would not survive. But have you ever thought about how your blood might shape your health, or about the different types that exist?

Many people feel squeamish around blood. A single drop turns them pale. Still, blood is nothing to fear. It is a substance that supports life, helps us live longer, and offers scientists a window into the human past.

Blood types reveal more about human history than almost any other scientific clue. In this case, blood is far more than just blood.

Blood keeps us alive, and blood types can teach us how to feel better day to day.

In this summary, Dr. Peter D'Adamo suggests that certain foods may suit certain blood groups better than others. He also notes that the food you eat reacts with your blood. When the two clash, your body cannot pull the full nutrition from the meal.

You may not know your blood type today, but after reading this content you will likely want to find out. Once you do, you can shape your habits around it and feel your best, getting the most out of every bite for your overall health.

This summary will walk you through the main blood types, how each reacts with food, and what those reactions tell us about human history. By the end, you will see that blood is far more than red liquid moving through your body. You will understand its real meaning, and you may even grow curious about this vital substance.

chapters.title: How blood and certain substances have fascinated people for centuries
chapters.text:
It all started with the discovery of blood transfusions, which gave results ranging from miraculous to disastrous.

In 1888, a researcher named Herman Stillmark decided to stop using animals in his experiments. He switched to cell cultures, and soon noticed something. When he mixed red blood cells with extracts from castor seeds, the cells clumped together. Scientists call this agglutination.

He went on to find more substances that caused this clumping and helped push the field of immunology forward.

The first big discoveries about blood happened in 1888, almost by accident.

Building on the earlier work of German bacteriologist Pat Erlich, Dr. Karl Landsteiner identified the ABO blood group antigens and antibodies. He earned a Nobel Prize in 1930 for this work. He saw that red blood cells from some people clumped while others did not, and from this he worked out the A, B, AB, and O groups.

Then, in 1945, William Clouser Boyd, Ph.D., made another fascinating find. He showed that some agglutinins only worked on specific blood groups, clumping one type while leaving others alone. The agglutinin in lima beans, for example, only clumped type A cells and did nothing to the rest.

Finding blood groups became easier once scientists had the right chemicals to work with.

Anthropologists began to use these tools and ideas to learn more about our shared past. Most knowledge of early humans came from written records, but to go back further, before writing existed, scientists had to look at other clues.

Time wears objects down, especially our bodies, so we have lost a great deal of information about the past. It is as if the story of our species is a book with only a few pages filled in.

However, science and decades of careful work have given us a useful tool to read what came before.

A regular, intense exercise routine lifts the spirit and helps type Os stay at a healthy weight, keep their emotions steady, and build strong self-esteem, in the words of Dr. Peter J. D'Adamo.

chapters.title: Biological markers like blood groups reveal deep truths about our past
chapters.text:
Each blood type handles food in its own way and reacts differently to outside threats such as parasites. Different types also come with different levels of risk, which helps explain why blood types are spread the way they are today. You could almost call it a map for reading the past.

Blood reacts differently with local bacteria depending on the group.

Almost every major infectious disease that spread before antibiotics had a preference for one ABO group over the others.

Many microbes have their own versions of ABO blood types. This can cause trouble. For example, a bacterium with a surface antigen that looks like the A antigen would slip into a person with type A blood more easily.

Each blood type has had its moment in history, when conditions favored it. Type O rose as humans climbed to the top of the food chain. Type A spread as hunters and gatherers settled into farming communities. Types B and AB appeared later, as people moved and mixed across regions.

In the 1960s, the author's father, James D'Adamo, N.D., was the first to put forward the idea that ABO blood groups could point to the best diet for each person.

He saw that most of his patients did well on a low-fat vegetarian diet, yet a small group did not improve or even got worse. He wanted to know why, and he spent his life looking for the answer.

After years of watching patients, a pattern appeared. Those with type A blood thrived on vegetarian meals, while those with type O felt their best on animal foods, especially meat. James D'Adamo put his findings into a book called One Man's Food. Inspired by his father's work, Dr. Peter D'Adamo spent twenty years digging deeper, focusing on blood groups, specific diets, and how they shape digestion and immunity.

We can match a diet to each blood group.

chapters.title: Chemical analysis shows that the O antigen is the simplest of the blood groups
chapters.text:
Many studies over the years have looked at blood groups. Several suggested that humans were all type O for most of our history. The high rate of type O in old and isolated populations points to how ancient this group really is.

Many theories of human evolution link the growth of the brain to a shift toward meat-rich eating. As humans got smarter, they became better hunters, and big game offered plenty of protein-rich meat. As we evolved, we got better at bringing down prey rich in protein, thanks to our growing intelligence.

In this stage, humans had a meat-heavy diet, and the digestive traits of type O were a perfect match. Strong stomach acid and pepsin made meat easy to break down.

Large game in Africa started to thin out, and that pushed early human ancestors to move in search of food. As they spread, they carried type O blood with them.

Humans are prone to certain illnesses simply because of their blood groups.

Every blood group has its strengths, its weak spots, and conditions where it does well or poorly.

The main drawbacks of type O include a higher risk of inflammatory diseases, which often hit older people. The type O stomach handles meat well, but it tends toward ulcers because of the strong hydrochloric acid in it. Type O also has a higher rate of thyroid disease and hyperthyroidism than other ABO groups.

The strengths of type O include a low rate of gallstones, jaundice, and liver cirrhosis. It also sees less influenza and very low rates of gum disease.

Dr. Peter D'Adamo says that some foods are harmful for certain blood groups and very helpful for others.

Here are some examples of useful diets for type O. Beneficial foods include beef, buffalo, lamb, liver, mutton, veal, venison, and bass. Neutral foods include duck egg, black bean, lima bean, soybean, tofu, hazelnut, and sesame seed. Harmful foods include cheese, ice cream, milk, yogurt, whey, peanut and peanut butter, corn, beer, coffee, and liquor.

One useful note: Dr. Peter D'Adamo points out that about one in five patients admitted to the hospital will need blood.

chapters.title: Civilization and the growth of cities go hand in hand with the rise of blood group A
chapters.text:
Big game grew scarce, and people learned to farm grains and raise livestock. This shift pulled humans away from their wandering lifestyle and pushed them toward a more settled one.

A species that had once moved freely now had to adjust to life in organized communities. Cities sprang up, and people lived close together for the first time. The big change in diet led to a new mutation in the digestive and immune systems. Carriers of blood group A began to appear because the new conditions favored them.

A small change in what you eat can have a large effect on how you feel.

Over time, humans lost some of their ability to handle the meat-heavy diet of the pre-agricultural era.

As Dr. Peter J. D'Adamo notes, the blood group A variant helped people digest grains and other farm products more easily.

People with blood group A are also more resistant to infections common in crowded areas.

As people moved from a meat-heavy diet to one based on farming, their stomachs produced less acid. They did not need strong acid to digest vegetables, grains, and farm-raised animals, so they had fewer ulcers. In older age, type A people often see their stomach acid drop further, which can lead to digestive issues.

Hepatitis and common tropical liver infections that cause scarring show up more often in type A than in any other group. Pseudomonas aeruginosa, the germ behind swimmer's ear, also has a preference for type A blood. Childhood ear infections follow a similar pattern.

Here are some beneficial and harmful foods for type A. Beneficial choices include carp, cod, monkfish, perch, sardine, salmon, snail, fava bean, black-eye pea, adzuki bean, lentil, soybean, peanut and peanut butter, artichoke, oat, rye, carrot, and collard greens. Neutral choices include jicama, chicken, grouse, bass, butterfish, mullet, pike, tilapia, farmer cheese, feta cheese, goat cheese, yogurt, egg, and almonds. Harmful choices include bacon, beef, buffalo, duck, mutton, veal, venison, lobster, cheese, whey, cashew, Brazil nut, wheat, mushroom, and olive.

chapters.title: Blood group B stands apart because climate change shaped it
chapters.text:
Humans began on the flat, tropical savannah, but overhunting and overcrowding pushed them to look for new food sources. They started to move.

The cold, dry, mountainous parts of the Indian subcontinent and the empty plains of the Central Asian steppes were tough places. These conditions triggered the genetic changes that led to blood group B. This makes B unique, because it is the only group that came about this way.

Blood group B grew and spread because of the harsh climates its early carriers faced.

It is fair to say that blood group B may have been the only group able to handle such a tough environment. The early carriers of this gene were often nomads. They depended on herding animals and dairy. We cannot say that everyone who lived this way was type B, but many of the strongest survivors likely were.

Blood group B has the traits needed to thrive in hard settings, but it has its weak spots, like every other group. Evidence shows that this type is more likely to develop unusual, slow-moving viral and neurological problems, including ALS. Type B also runs a higher risk of cerebral aneurysm and asthma. There is no guarantee a person with type B will face these issues, but the risk is higher than for other groups.

On the positive side, this group sees less arthritis, less fibromyalgia, and lower rates of some cancers.

Here are some harmful and beneficial foods for type B. Beneficial foods include goat, lamb, mutton, rabbit, venison, caviar, croaker, cod, mackerel, sardine, cottage cheese, goat cheese, milk, yogurt, kidney bean, lima bean, millet, oat, rice, beet, and carrot. Neutral foods include beef, buffalo, liver, pheasant, turkey, carp, herring, mullet, perch, butter, wheat, chicken egg, snap bean, soybean, almond, and barley. Harmful foods include bacon, chicken, duck, goose, veal, bass, crab, lobster, egg, lentil, wheat, olive, radish, and tomato.

chapters.title: Why blood group AB stands out as the newest of the four
chapters.text:
We turn now to AB, the rarest of the four. Many researchers call AB the most recent blood group, and some even see it as a human creation. AB appeared when A and B carriers crossed paths, through migration or invasion, and their genes combined in their children.

One of AB's defining features is its wide tolerance. Because it carries both A and B antigens, it treats anything A-like or B-like as part of itself. The downside is that AB carriers run the highest cancer rate of any group. The bright side is that viruses often have a smaller effect on AB, perhaps because it formed later and in a different way from the older groups.

Every blood group has trade-offs, but the heaviest one for AB may be the higher risk of heart disease. That makes a thoughtful diet and regular exercise especially important for AB carriers. By choosing good foods and steering clear of the ones that tend to cause trouble, you give yourself a real chance at a longer, healthier life.

AB carriers often handle allergies and autoimmune conditions better than other types.

The Hepatitis B virus antigen is often missing in AB blood, which leaves AB carriers more open to the harshest forms of that disease.

AB brings together the strengths and weaknesses of A and B in one package.

To round out the picture, here are some foods that suit AB well and some that do not. These can help reduce risks and bring out the benefits of this rare type. Strong choices include turkey, cod, grouper, monkfish, pike, pickerel, salmon, sardine, snail, tuna, cottage cheese, milk, yogurt, lentil, walnut, millet, oat, and rye. Neutral choices include kelp, ginger, cucumber juice, carrot, agar, wheat, barley, flaxseed, cashew, Brazil nut, almond milk, and white beans. Foods to limit include beef, chicken, duck, buffalo, crab, haddock, oyster, shrimp, black-eye pea, kidney bean, lima bean, sorghum, and capers.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The ABO blood group gives a useful structure for thinking about personal diets, and the results so far have been broad and positive. Still, even with Dr. Peter D'Adamo's passion for the work, the wider scientific community has been slow to accept the theory because the supporting evidence is thin.

As of 2013, a review of thousands of studies on blood type diets found that not a single one was well-built and accurate. Many studies do show that the suggested diets, like the type A diet rich in vegetables and fruit, are good for health. But the benefit was not limited to any blood group. Nearly anyone eating that way became healthier.

So, while there is nothing wrong with the diets themselves, there is no strong proof of a blood type link. Even without that link, one thing is sure to boost your immune system: a regular exercise habit. Pair that with a healthy diet and you have the foundation for a balanced life.

There is no single diet that fits everyone. You may be a certain blood group and find the matching list does not work well for you. Maybe you have a sensitivity that clashes with the suggestions. If so, keeping a food journal will help you spot which foods lift you up and which hold you back. From there, you can shape a plan that truly fits you.

Try this. If you do not know your blood group, a quick visit to your doctor and a simple blood test will tell you. Different diets work for different people. A vegetarian plan might suit one person, while a meat-rich plan might suit another, so try a few foods and see what feels right. A food journal is a good way to track how your body reacts to different meals.

Did you read it all the way through?
